A breakdown of Data Pipelines in Machine Learning Systems. It is also applicable for LLM-based systems.

Ensuring Data Quality and Integrity upstream of ML Training and Inference Pipelines is critical. Addressing these issues downstream can lead to failures when operating at scale.

There is significant work to be done on the Data Lake or LakeHouse layer. Below is an example architecture for a production-grade end-to-end data flow:

1. Schema changes are implemented in version control. Once approved, they are pushed to the applications generating the data, databases holding the data, and a central Data Contract Registry.
2. Applications push generated data to Kafka Topics:
   - Events emitted directly by the Application Services, including IoT Fleets and Website Activity Tracking.
   - Raw Data Topics for CDC streams.
3. A Flink Application consumes data from Raw Data streams and validates it against schemas in the Contract Registry.
4. Data that does not meet the contract is pushed to a Dead Letter Topic.
5. Data that meets the contract is pushed to a Validated Data Topic.
6. Data from the Validated Data Topic is pushed to object storage for additional validation.
7. On a schedule, data in the Object Storage is validated against additional SLAs in Data Contracts and pushed to the Data Warehouse for transformation and modeling for analytical purposes.
8. Modeled and curated data is pushed to the Feature Store System for further feature engineering.
   - Real-time features are ingested into the Feature Store directly from the Validated Data Topic.
9. High-quality data is used in Machine Learning Training Pipelines.
10. The same data is used for Feature Serving in Inference.
Let me know your thoughts.

Is it just me, or are timelines stretching across the board right now? 

In conversations with clients, candidates, and peers, I’ve noticed a consistent theme this year: 

- Hiring processes are taking longer to finalize 
- Feedback cycles are a bit more drawn out 
- Contracts and agreements are taking longer to align and execute 
- Projects require more discussion before moving forward 

It doesn’t feel like a lack of urgency; rather, decisions today seem to carry more weight. 

A few factors contributing to this shift include: 

- More stakeholders involved across the board 
- Greater focus on long-term fit and ROI 
- Teams balancing more priorities than ever 
- The growing influence of AI—both as a tool and a factor in decision-making 

When you look at all the variables, it’s less about things slowing down and more about decisions being made with greater intention on all sides

Curious if others are seeing the same—are timelines truly extending, or just evolving?

Most staffing firms are losing placements to a capacity problem, not a performance problem.

A recruiter with 80 applications and time to call 20 will miss someone worth placing. Not because they're not good at their job. Because there aren't enough hours. The other 60 candidates don't disappear because they weren't qualified. They disappear because the week ended first.

Recruiters are measured on placements. But before the first placement can happen, someone has to sort through the full application pool and decide who deserves a conversation. That work takes time. A lot of it. And it produces nothing until a decision is made about who to call.

The problem compounds in high-volume staffing. More open roles, more applicants, more sorting, and the same number of hours in the week. The firms growing fastest are not hiring more recruiters. They're changing what recruiters spend their time on.

AI safety tends to get framed as something for the AI labs to worry about - alignment, existential risk, frontier models - and it feels like a different world from the day-to-day reality of most organisations.

When I completed my course with the Center for AI Safety earlier this year, my project focused on closing that gap and exploring where AI safety risks are relevant to everyday businesses. The risks AI safety researchers think about don't only apply at the frontier, and they frequently show up in everyday use cases such as AI hiring decisions, customer service chatbots, vendor contracts, and automated workflows too.

I've turned that project into an article detailing seven reasons why AI safety is already relevant to every organisation, each with a real-world example and how to start thinking about it. This article is only a brief introduction to the complex topic of AI safety, and please get in touch if you'd like to discuss any of this in more detail.

If you find my Red Giant articles interesting or useful, I'd really appreciate you sharing them with your network or subscribing to The Red Giant, where I write about how to use AI and the impact it's having on work and education.

Reducing working hours in Mexico 🇲🇽, is often framed as a social and well-being improvement.

That is valid.

But in labor-intensive sectors like contract logistics, it is critical to also understand the full operating and economic implications—because the impact is not isolated, it is systemic.

A gradual shift (e.g., 48 → 40 hours) does not generate a one-time disruption.  
It creates a rolling increase in unit labor cost.

Why?

Because fewer hours per worker—while maintaining service levels—requires a combination of:
• Additional hiring  
• Overtime (at premium rates)  
• Or productivity gains that are not always sufficient in the short term  

📌 Key insight #1 — The impact cascades

The effect does not stay in direct labor.

Functions often classified as SG&A—control towers, coordination, exception management—are also highly human and time-dependent.

They face the same constraints:
→ More people  
→ Or more overtime  

Result: a system-wide cost increase, not a contained one.

📌 Key insight #2 — Timing defines the outcome

At first, companies absorb the impact:
• Margin compression  
• Selective cost cuts  
• Delayed investments  

But this is temporary.

Costs adjust immediately.  
Prices adjust later.

That gap is where strategy—and risk—lives.

📌 Key insight #3 — You cannot cost-cut your way out

Cutting SG&A or discretionary spend helps… but only partially.

Eventually, companies are forced to act through:
• Repricing contracts  
• Redesigning operations  
• Rationalizing capacity  

This is when the effect becomes visible:
not as a spike, but as persistent inflationary pressure.

📌 Key insight #4 — The compounding effect

Now add:
• Ongoing minimum wage increases  
• In economies with moderate growth  

This amplifies the pressure on labor cost structures.

(This alone deserves a separate analysis.)

📌 Key insight #5 — Automation is not a free lever

Acceleration in:
• Automation  
• Digitalization  

…comes with trade-offs:
• Higher CAPEX  
• Increased depreciation  
• Maintenance costs  
• Execution risk  

The real decision is not “automate or not”  
→ but when, how fast, and under which return profile

📌 Key insight #6 — A question of timing

If the intention is to benefit the workforce,  
the reform is intersecting with a moment of accelerated AI adoption.

That tension matters.

🎯 The takeaway is not to oppose or support the reform.

It is to recognize that:
👉 multiple scenarios must be modeled

Companies will need to evaluate:
• Cost absorption vs repricing  
• Investment vs margin preservation  
• Service levels vs capacity constraints  

Because the system will adjust.

The real question is:

How prepared is each organization to navigate that transition?

I've talked to 250+ CROs about AI in GTM this year. Most are pointed at the wrong problem.

Here's the filter I'd use for any AI investment right now.

No contracts longer than 12 months. Not because vendors can't prove value in 12. Because you can't afford to give up optionality in a market that reprices every quarter.

No vendors founded before 2022. Easy filter against AI-pretenders. If the architecture was designed before LLMs were infrastructure, you're paying to bridge the gap. A few exceptions, Salesforce chief among them. Everyone else, skip.

No SDRs. Most CROs I talked to this year tried AI SDRs, watched them fail, and went back to hiring humans to do the same broken motion. The SDR-AE split was a response to manual limitations. AI dissolves the reason it existed.

No reps in the CRM for anything except the forecast. The time argument is obvious. The data argument matters more. Rep-logged activity is editorialized by definition. You're running pipeline reviews on data with opinions baked in. AI capture gives you signal without the spin.

No "AI tool" that's really just faster outbound. Volume with context is a strategy. Volume without context is a confession that you don't know which accounts to go after.

No GTM engineer as your AI bet. The real gains aren't in making the current motion run faster. They're in questioning whether the current motion should exist. Most GTM engineers are doing the first one.

None of this is incremental. That's the point. If your AI roadmap fits inside your current org chart, you're not making a bet. You're buying insurance.

Hiring demand hasn’t slowed. 
Hiring speed still has.

Across our client base in software engineering, data and AI, the same pattern is playing out.

Projects are moving quickly.
Hiring processes are not.

Permanent hiring cycles are still averaging 6–10 weeks in many cases.

In that time, priorities shift, candidates move, and delivery slows.

As a result, more teams are turning to contract hiring to bridge that gap. Not as a replacement for permanent hiring, but as a way to keep momentum.

The companies moving fastest right now are not choosing one model.
They are structuring both to work together.

We’re seeing a clear shift in how contract is being used across 2026.

The 3 biggest software PE firms in the world just held their LP meetings.
$353 billion in assets under management. 190+ portfolio companies. And they can't agree on whether AI will kill their investments.
𝗩𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮 ($𝟭𝟴𝟯𝗕): "The market's wrong. Mission-critical SaaS is insulated from AI. Nobody's replacing hospital billing with a chatbot."
𝗩𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮 𝗘𝗾𝘂𝗶𝘁𝘆 ($𝟭𝟬𝟬𝗕): "Incumbents can win — but only if they stop leaking IP through bad customer contracts and start building around data sovereignty."
𝗞𝗼𝘁𝘂 ($𝟳𝟬𝗕): "The entire SaaS pricing model is being rewritten. We're shifting from $200B of per-seat software to $5.5 trillion in services-as-software."
Meanwhile, Workday's CTO just left. Gave up a C-suite title to become a "member of technical staff" at Anthropic. Took a demotion to go build the HR software that will replace his last company.
Here's what nobody's saying out loud:
The question isn't whether AI disrupts your SaaS stack. It's whether the disruption happens TO you or FOR you.
If you're running Salesforce, ServiceNow, HubSpot — those tools aren't going away. Vista's right about that. But the way you manage them is about to change completely.
Right now you're paying $150/hr consultants to do health checks. Hiring admins to deploy metadata changes. Spending weeks on what should take minutes.
That's the $5.5T opportunity Kotu is talking about. Not replacing the software. Replacing the human labor around the software with AI agents that actually understand your org.
That's what we built Buildforce to do. Not another SaaS tool. An AI layer that sits on top of your existing stack and does the work.
Your Salesforce org gets a health check in minutes, not weeks. Fixes deploy automatically. Your team stops babysitting platforms and starts building on them.
The Workday CTO saw the future and left to build it. Are you going to wait until your vendor figures it out, or take control now?

AI and The Future In Law.

Automation of Routine Tasks: AI handles the heavy lifting of document review, legal research, and contract analysis. This shifts the lawyer's role from "data processor" to high-level strategic advisor.

Predictive Analytics: By analyzing years of case law, AI can predict the likely outcome of a trial or how a specific judge might rule. This helps firms manage litigation risk and settle cases more efficiently.

Increased Access to Justice: Low-cost, AI-driven legal bots can help people navigate simple issues like traffic tickets or small claims without hiring an expensive attorney, narrowing the justice gap.

Virtual Courts and Digital Evidence: AI supports the move toward "Online Dispute Resolution" (ODR), where smaller cases are handled entirely digitally, reducing backlogs in the physical court system.

Ethical and Regulatory Challenges: The legal system must now grapple with "black box" algorithms, data privacy, and the risk of algorithmic bias in sentencing or hiring, leading to new laws specifically for AI oversight.

Part 2:


This time bit cruel from my end but truth is to revealed by some one and let me start with 🎓 The "Degree Is Useless" Era – A Love Letter to India's New Grads 💔


🏭 Fastest-Growing Industries – The Good, Bad & Ugly

Utilities and Education are hiring fresh talent like crazy.

But:

1) Utilities:

Growing at 47% YoY, but 30% of roles are contractual, not permanent. 😟

2) Education:

EdTech is booming (₹29,000 crore market by 2025), but 1 in 3 edtech employees report burnout 📚🔥😫


"You'll get a job. Whether you'll keep it beyond 6 months? That's the real question." – Rajesh Kumar, HR Analyst 🤒


💼 The "In-Demand" Functions Trap

HR and Consulting lead the charts.

But let me translate:

1) HR: 4.2 lakh openings projected, but 62% require AI/digital skills you don't have yet.

2) Consulting: Entry-level hiring dropped 18% in 2024 vs 2023.

The "glamour" is fading 🎭

1) Profit: High demand, decent starting salaries (₹6-12 LPA)

2) Loss: Ridiculous competition – 300+ applicants per role on average


📉 The Losses You're Neglecting (And Why That Hurts)

1) Mental Health:

68% of young Indian professionals report anxiety due to job instability.

2) Financial Drain:

₹20 lakh degree + ₹1 lakh certification + 6 months unpaid internship = negative ROI for first 3 years.

3) Skill Obsolescence:

47% of Indian graduates are "unemployable" due to outdated curricula.

4) The Gig Trap:

55% of new hires are on contract/ gig basis – no PF, no medical, no stability.

5) Comparison Hell:

Watching classmates succeed while you struggle = 🧠💥


📈 The Profits (Yes, There Are Some) 🎉

1) Remote Work:

40% of Indian companies now offer hybrid/remote for freshers – save ₹50k/year on commute 🚗💨

2) AI-Augmented Roles:

70% of jobs will be "AI-collaborative" by 2027 – learn tools, not theories 🤖

3) Tier-2 Boom:

15 lakh new jobs expected in smaller cities by 2026 – less competition, more breathing room

4) Upskilling Platforms:

Coursera/Udemy saw 300% growth in Indian users – cheap, fast, relevant (if you choose wisely)


"The degree gets you the interview. The portfolio gets you the job. The adaptability keeps you employed." – Neha Sharma, Career Coach


P.S. – If this post made you smile even a little, share it with a fellow grad.

Misery loves company, but success loves teamwork. 😉

What does it take to recruit a lawyer in the age of AI?

We all use AI. That’s not something to hide, it’s quickly becoming a basic professional skill. But it does change how you assess people.
Written answers can now be clear, structured and persuasive, yet still leave open the question of how deep the underlying understanding really is. The quality of output is no longer a reliable proxy for quality of thinking.

The focus shifts to:
·      How someone communicates in a live discussion, without a tool to rely on. 
·      Whether they can actually run a negotiation, adapt their position, and explain it simply. 
·      Whether they understand a contract as a whole, not just as a series of well-drafted clauses.

There is also a question of readiness. AI can make candidates sound more polished than they really are. It takes more effort to understand what sits behind that, how independently someone can operate, and where they still need development.

None of this is a criticism of AI. It’s part of how we work now.
But it does mean we need to look beyond the output, and spend more time understanding how people think.

🎯 AI Series - Part Three: Risk 🎯

I ask business owners one simple question:

"What's your plan when your AI makes a bad call?"

The silence in the room says everything.

AI risk isn't a future problem. It's a right-now problem, and most companies are walking into it wide open.

Here's what's being ignored:
📜 Legal liability is yours, not your vendor's
➡️ Most AI contracts clearly state the vendor isn't responsible for outcomes
➡️ You own the result. Always.
➡️ Has your legal team reviewed your AI deployment? Make that call.

⚖️ AI decisions can discriminate...unintentionally
➡️ Hiring. Lending. Customer service. Operations.
➡️ Biased outputs happen without anyone intending it
➡️ Regulators aren't waiting for companies to catch up

🔐 Your AI is a cybersecurity target
➡️ AI systems can be manipulated and exploited
➡️ Most security teams aren't in the room when AI is deployed
➡️ They need to be before go-live, not after a breach

🚨 The question that changes everything:

"Who is accountable when AI makes a bad call, and what's our rollback plan?"

Risk planning isn't pessimism. It's leadership.

The companies that build AI responsibly — with clear accountability and guardrails are the ones that earn long-term trust from customers, employees, and regulators.

Move fast. But know what's in place before you do.

💬 Has your team had an honest conversation about AI risk yet?

I keep having the same conversation with event business owners.

"We just don't have time for consistent marketing."

They're right. They don't. Here's exactly why.

A 2024 Slack survey found business owners lose an average of 96 minutes of productive time every single day.

Not to big problems. To the constant pull of operational work that crowds out everything else.

In event businesses, the gap is even more stark. Because the operational load doesn't just eat the owner's time. It swallows the whole team's.

Here's where the hours are actually going:

𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐌𝐔𝐍𝐈𝐂𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍𝐒 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐃𝐎𝐂𝐔𝐌𝐄𝐍𝐓𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍
Email threads, status updates, timeline revisions, briefing docs.
Eats 20-30% of total planning time before a single event has even been confirmed.

𝐕𝐄𝐍𝐃𝐎𝐑 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐋𝐎𝐆𝐈𝐒𝐓𝐈𝐂𝐒 𝐌𝐀𝐍𝐀𝐆𝐄𝐌𝐄𝐍𝐓
Quote comparisons, contract negotiations, AV coordination, F&B, travel, floor plans, rooming lists.
Another 30-40% of your team's bandwidth. Every. Single. Event.

𝐑𝐄𝐆𝐈𝐒𝐓𝐑𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍, 𝐃𝐀𝐓𝐀 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐏𝐋𝐈𝐀𝐍𝐂𝐄
Form building, list management, dietary and accessibility requirements, risk documentation, H&S paperwork.
Add another 15-25% on top of everything else.

And that's just the recurring operational load.

It doesn't include the reactive stuff. The supplier who goes quiet two weeks out. The last-minute scope change. The client who needs three more revisions on the run of show.

So when does the marketing happen?

When does someone sit down to write the LinkedIn post, build the email sequence, brief the Google campaign, nurture the warm lead who enquired six weeks ago and hasn't heard back since?

The honest answer: it doesn't. Not consistently. Not in a way that compounds.

And then the quiet months arrive. The calendar looks thin. The referrals slow down. And the same conversation starts again.

"We just haven't had time to focus on marketing."

The event businesses that break this pattern aren't working harder. They're not hiring faster.

They're systematically identifying which of these administrative pieces can be handled by automation, AI, or a proper system — and giving that time back to the work that actually fills a calendar.

Not perfectly. Not overnight.

But intentionally. And it compounds.

What percentage of your week goes to admin versus the work you actually got into this industry to do?

🤖 Stop Hiring "Chatbots" When You Need A Full-Time Employee

🚨 Imagine it’s 2026. A high-value lead lands on your site at 2 AM. They’re caffeinated, they’ve got a budget, and they’re ready to solve a problem now.

🎪 But your "AI" is just a lonely, generic bubble that says: "How can I help you?" only to follow up with: "Thanks! A human will email you in 2-3 business days."

📉 By then, that lead has already moved on, signed a contract with your competitor, and probably forgotten you even exist. You didn't have AI. You had a digital paperweight acting as a "Do Not Disturb" sign for your business.

The Relatable Pain: The "Human" Bottleneck

🥂 Look, I love humans. Some of my best friends are humans. But humans are biologically expensive. They need sleep. They get stuck in "meetings about a meeting." They get burned out answering "What’s your pricing?" for the 400th time.

🧘‍♂️❌ When a lead has a fire in their belly to buy, you don’t give them a "we'll get back to you" form. That’s like handing a starving person a menu and telling them the chef is on a 3-day spiritual retreat.

💎 The 2026 Standard: The AI Agent

✅ Operation: An AI Agent doesn't just "chat." It operates.
✅ Scheduling: It doesn't just take a message; it checks your real-time calendar and books the meeting.
✅ Logic: It doesn't just guess; it qualifies the lead based on your specific sales logic and budget tiers.
🎙️ Voice: It handles discovery calls with a voice so natural it's almost scary.
✅ Reliability: It works 24/7/365 with zero coffee breaks and a 100% follow-up rate.

The Reality Check:

⚖️ Your competitors aren't out-hiring you; they’re out-automating you. While you’re paying a massive salary for data entry and basic triage, they’re using AI Agents to handle 90% of the heavy lifting for the cost of a nice dinner. They are scaling their revenue while you are still "managing" your inbox.

Our Offer:

💸 We don't build "bots." We build Digital Employees.
🛠️ We create fully integrated AI Voice and Chat Agents that bridge the gap between "Inquiry" and "Invoice." We build the brain of your business so you can go back to being the heart of it.

✨ If you’re ready to stop managing "tasks" and start managing results, let’s talk. Our agents don't just talk to people; they move the needle. You focus on the strategy; let our agents handle the grunt work.

🚀 The future is automated. Don't be the one holding the manual.

💰 Stop leaving money on the table because of a "3-day response time." The 2026 market doesn't wait, and neither should your business. Let's build your first Digital Employee today.

“I wasn’t sure if I was allowed to use AI in the take home…” 

A candidate said that to me this week. I said… why wouldn’t you?

In the job, you’re using it every day. So why would a company assess you without it?

Right now I’m running 6 hiring processes, high-impact contracts + senior FTE. AI is part of the assessment (take-home / live coding). 

Candidates are being assessed on how they use it, not whether they avoid it. This isn’t my opinion.

It’s what I’m seeing from clients moving fastest:
→ USA consumer platform, Series C.
→ USA health tech, Series A.
→ One of Europe’s fastest-growing AI platforms, Series D.

All are doing the same thing and that is not restricting AI during the interview process.  They’re testing for real, applied, agentic usage.

Which makes sense… because every client right now is asking for it.

So it does it really make sense to block it in the interview process? This is a huge mindset shift I've seen in the last 6 months.

Different story if someone is relying on ChatGPT to answer technical questions live- that’s a HUGE red flag. 🚩🚩

But using AI properly as part of how you build? That’s the job now.

Curious, are you seeing the same shift in your hiring processes?
Or are companies still trying to control it?

Three bubbles. Two we measured. One still forming.

We queried 48.7 million workforce records across 4.2 million companies. Here is what two economic crises actually looked like at the company level.

The dot-com crash was a sector story:

In 2000, internet companies were growing at 27.5% per year. Computer software at 30.6%. Telecom at 30.3%. By 2002, internet dropped to 6.5%. Software to 11.6%. Telecom to 11.3%. Contraction rates doubled across all three sectors. But retail barely moved. 12.2% growth in 2000. 9.6% in 2002. The bubble was surgical. It destroyed the companies closest to the hype and left everyone else standing.
Net hiring collapsed 34% in a single year. From 1.35 million net new positions in 2000 to 895,000 in 2001. Across 777,000 companies.

COVID was an everything story:

Every industry flipped negative in the same year. Computer software went from +4.9% to -1.8%. Internet from +3.0% to -2.2%. Financial services from +4.8% to -1.4%. Staffing and recruiting from +4.7% to -2.2%. Net hiring swung from +440,000 to -833,000. A 1.27 million position reversal across 2.8 million companies. No sector was spared.

The AI bubble is still forming. The question is which pattern it follows.
Does it correct like the dot-com crash, where only AI-native companies contract while everyone else holds steady? Or does it hit like COVID, where the downstream effects ripple through every industry at once? Or does it keep growing, defying the pattern entirely?

We do not know yet. But we captured what both previous bubbles looked like at the company level. Year by year. Industry by industry. Role by role. Across millions of companies.

The models trained on this data have already seen both patterns. They will recognize the next one before the headlines catch up.

🚀 When Workflow Automation meets AI, the real unlock for businesses
For years, my work revolved around designing business workflows and repetitive tasks into reliable, predictable systems.
But something has fundamentally changed.
With AI in the loop, automation is no longer just "executing steps." Systems can now analyze, decide, and recommend, while humans stay in control of what matters most.
I recently built an AI-Native Workflow Automation Platform to prove exactly this idea 👇
The use case: a real business scenario, subscription & contract management (energy contracts in this demo, but the pattern applies to insurance, telecom, SaaS, procurement, and more).
The flow:
Input Validation → Data Normalization → Automated Competitor Scraping → AI Analysis → Decision → Human-in-the-Loop Approval → Execution
Under the hood:
🔹 .NET 8 + Clean Architecture + CQRS (maintainable, testable, production-ready)
🔹 Angular 17 frontend with a clean operator UI
🔹 Microsoft Semantic Kernel + OpenAI for the AI reasoning layer
🔹 Windmill for distributed workflow execution (with graceful fallback to in-process .NET)
🔹 Playwright for browser automation & data scraping
🔹 PostgreSQL + structured logging with Serilog
🔹 Deterministic fallback logic when AI is unavailable, so the business never stops
Why this matters 👇
Most companies, from 10-person startups to 10,000-person enterprises, are still drowning in repetitive work:
⚙️ Invoice & document processing
⚙️ Customer onboarding
⚙️ Contract analysis & renewals
⚙️ Vendor comparison & procurement
⚙️ Internal reporting
⚙️ Email triage & classification
The combination of Workflow Orchestration + AI + Human-in-the-Loop can automate 70–90% of these tasks, without removing human judgment from critical decisions.

If you're a founder, hiring manager, operations lead, or CTO, I'd love to hear what you're working on and how I can help.
